Description 1: We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the Board breached the law by failing to submit the audited financial statements to the Ministry of Education by 31 May 2023.

We disagreed with the Board not complying with the Property Occupancy Document which requires the Board to prepare and annually review a property plan that includes all the maintenance requirements of the school over a continuous ten-year period. The school has not prepared its long-term maintenance planning and not obtained an independent assessment by a qualified person in the last three years.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school has not adjusted the provision for cyclical maintenance although it has been relocated to a new building in April 2022. The impact of the relocation on the provision for cyclical maintenance could not be reliably estimated.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the Board spent $24,426 on hospitality and gifts. This expenditure includes $ 10,671 for the previous principal's farewell ($5,610 of which was spent on jackets for the staff for school photos), $2,033 on gifts for the principal, and $5,611 for the new build opening. Although the expenditure was approved by the school Board, it was relatively high. It should be both moderate and conservative and appropriate to the occasion.

We highlighted that the school breached legislation by enrolling and meeting the costs of one international student in the year ended 31 December 203 through a scholarship. Legislation requires the school charge fees for international students that are not less than the estimated costs of providing tuition to one student in the relevant subject, course, or programme including the provision of capital facilities, plus any other fees prescribed for international students.
We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the Board breached the law by failing to submit the audited financial statements to the Ministry of Education by 31 May 2021.

Our audit was limited because we were unable to determine the fair value of the interest in Napier High Schools Land Endowment Trusts (the Endowment Trust), a third of which is owned by Napier Boys High School Charitable Trust, an entity controlled by the school. The Endowment Trust uses the fair value model, in keeping with generally accepted accounting practice (GAAP), to measure its investments. The investment properties were valued during the year using their respective quotable values, which is contrary to the requirement of GAAP, to reflect market conditions at the reporting date. The Group did not determine the difference between the quotable value and the current market value, which is expected to be material.

The school's financial statements are prepared on a going concern basis. The going concern assumption is dependent on the continuing support from the Ministry of Education. The Ministry of Education has confirmed it will continue to provide the school with resources so it may meet its obligations as they fall due. We also drew attention to disclosures outlining uncertainties over the possible effects of the lockdown response to Covid-19 on the school. The school's financial statements also referred to a breach of legislation. Section 155 of the Education and Training Act 2020 and clause 12 of the Crown Entities (Financial Powers) Regulations 2005 permit a Board to borrow within a specified limit. Boards need Ministerial approval to exceed this limit. During the year repayments of loans exceeded this limit. These transactions had not received Ministerial approval.

Description 1: Our audit was limited because we could not get enough assurance about the fundraising revenue. The Board  had limited controls over that revenue.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining two matters. First, the school is experiencing serious financial difficulties. The school has incurred several deficits over recent years and relies on support from the Ministry of Education to meet its obligations as they fall due. Last, during the Limited Statutory Manager's review of the school's finance, some spending on the credit card during 2023 were identified not to have a clear business purpose.

We highlighted that the school reimbursed the former principal $,9000 (of funding tagged to be spent on the principal's professional coaching and wellbeing support) for a trip to Europe to visit and gain insightsfrom  some schools . As there was no detailed breakdown of the expenditure incurred or receipts to support the expenditure and no evidence of the board's approval, there was no clear business purpose for the spending linked to the school's objectives. It was also not clear whether the funds were spent in accordance with the school's policies and the expectations for the use of public funds. In addition, the school breached the law for not keeping accounting records that correctly explain the transactions.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that during 2024, the school and group dissolved the Auckland Grammar School Combined Trust (the Combined Trust). $2 million of the Combined Trust's assets (previously reported on as part of Auckland Grammar's Group financial statements) were transferred, as a donation, to the Auckland Grammar School Headmasters Council. At 31 December 2024, the Combined Trust has no assets or liabilities. The Auckland Grammar School financial statements do not include information about the financial transactions and balances of the Headmasters Council, because it is not controlled by the school board and is not a public entity.

We highlighted that the school paid $9,434 for a trip for the principal to Rarotonga from the school's 2024 professional coaching and wellbeing support funding. However, the school was unable to provide evidence of the business purpose for this spending or how it related to the school's objectives. Paying for personal travel of a staff member  is not consistent with the school’s travel policy.

We drew attention to the fact that during 2021 the Board discovered a misappropriation of funds. These funds were subsequently returned to the school in 2025. As this occurred before the 2021 financial statements were prepared, the school has adjusted the financial statements to reflect the fact that the misappropriated funds were recoverable.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school is experiencing serious financial difficulties. The school has incurred several deficits over recent years and relies on support from the Ministry of Education to meet its obligations as they fall due.

We highlighted that the school approved and paid  $5,000 of the school's operational funding, tagged to be spent on the principal's professional coaching and wellbeing support toward the principal and spouse's overseas trip. However, there was no clear business purpose for the spending and receipts were not provided. It was therefore not possible to assess whether the funds were spent according with expectations for the use of public funds and the school's travel policy. In addition, the Board has no requested concurrence from the Ministry of Education for the additonal payment to the principal and breached the law for not keeping accounting records to correctly explain the transactions of the school.
We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school had appropriately prepared the financial statements on a disestablishment basis because the school is closing on 15 December 2025.
We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school had appropriately prepared the financial statements on a disestablishment basis because the school was closed as at 27 June 2025.
We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school had appropriately prepared the financial statements on a disestablishment basis because the school was closed as at 27 June 2025.

We highlighted that the school paid $12,000 for travel to a four-day conference in Vancouver for the principal and his spouse from the school's professional coaching and wellbeing support funding. However, because there were no detailed receipts to support the expenditure, it was not possible to assess whether there was a clear business purpose for this spending, and whether the funds have been spent in accordance with the expectations for the use of public funds. Paying for personal travel of a staff member and family members is not consistent with the school’s travel policy.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school is experiencing serious financial difficulties. The school has negative working capital due to the Board's undertaking to develop the playground. The school relies on support from the Ministry of Education (MOE) to meet its obligations as they fall due. The MOE considers the school has appropriately prepared the financial statements on a going concern assumption.

We highlighted that the school failed to comply with the law by not seeking authority from the Ministers of Education and Finance for borrowing which, in aggregate, involves repayment of interest and capital in excess of one tenth of the school's operational activities grant for the year.

We highlighted that the school paid $17,155 for the principal to travel to Alaska, and Canada to research challenges facing indigenous peoples in education. Expenditure incurred included about $2,900 on accommodation and other expenses in Hawaii and New York. While the principal’s travel through Alaska and Canada was for the purposes of research, and therefore consistent with the purposes of the funding, the school did not provide sufficient evidence that all expenditure incurred had a clear business purpose, in particular spending during the stopovers in Hawaii, and New York, and spending on tourist activities in Canada and Alaska. In addition, spending that confers or could potentially confer a personal benefit for the principal requires concurrence from the Ministry of Education.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that following the principal’s passing in February 2024, the school made a contribution to his family of $11,035 which included unspent professionl and wellbeing support funding.

We highlighted that the school spent $29,458 on the principal's overseas travel to Canada for the Ulead Conference in Montreal, to Vietnam with the Presiding Member of the board for marketing the school, and to Samoa for conducting a site visit for a potential future kapa haka trip. These trips had a business purpose consistent with the school's strategy but there were not enough evidence that all the expenditure incurred had a clear business purpose or was spent in accordance with the expectations for the use of public funds. The school breached the law for not keeping accounting records to correctly explain the transactions of the school.
We drew attention to the fact that the school faced significant uncertainty over property maintenance because the school is to be rebuilt and a masterplan is being prepared for this rebuild.

We highlighted that the school paid $18,500 for a trip to Queenstown for Senior Leadership Team members funded partly from the school's 2023 and 2024 professional coaching and wellbeing support funding for the principal. The purpose of the trip was to visit a local school which is part of the Private Public Partnership network and as a team building opportunity. Expenditure incurred included about $10,000 on accommodation, and $6,000 on meals, drinks, and tourist activities in Queenstown. While the school has explained that there was a business purpose for the trip, the school did not provide sufficient evidence that all expenditure incurred as part of the trip had a clear business purpose. In addition, the school's professional coaching and wellbeing support funding was used not for the purposes for which it was provided.

We highlighted that the school spent a total of $8,527 on the principal's professional coaching and wellbeing support on overseas travel. This amount includes $6,000 for for the current principal's overseas travel and $2,527 for the previous principal's trip to Samoa. While the Board approved the expenditure, there were no detailed receipts to provide evidence for a clear business purpose, or that spending was consistent with the school's policies or the expectations for the use of public funds. The Board has not requested concurrence from the Ministry of Education for the additional benefits to the staff above those set out in the collective agreement. The school breached the law for not keeping accounting records to correctly explain the transactions of the school.
We highlighted that the school breached the law by enrolling and meeting the cost of one international student during the year through a scholarship.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school is experiencing serious financial difficulties. The school has incurred an operating deficit during the year and relies on support from the Ministry of Education to meet its obligations as they fall due.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining two matters. First, the school is experiencing serious financial difficulties. The school has incurred a deficit during the year and relies on support from the Ministry of Education to meet its obligations as they fall due. Last, the Board failed to comply with the law by leasing a property to an external party without obtaining the required consent from the Minister of Education.

We drew attention to disclosures explaining that during 2024 the School discovered a misappropriation of funds, relating to transactions between 2019-2024. The funds were returned to the school in 2024. We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the Board had appropriately prepared the financial statements on a disestablishment basis because the school was merged with Gore High School on 1 January 2024.

We highlighted that the school paid $9,156 from the school's 2024 professional coaching and wellbeing support funding for the principal to travel to Rarotonga and French Polynesia. Some of the spending was not adequately supported by receipts. The Board approved the expenditure but the school was unable to provide evidence of the business purpose for this spending or how it related to the school's objectives. We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school had appropriately prepared the financial statements on a disestablishment basis because the school was closed on 27 January 2024.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school breached the law by paying directly to teachers outside the central payroll system. The school is liaising with the Ministry and taking remedial action to ensure any PAYE and penalties are paid to the Inland Revenue department and to ensure there are no further breaches of legislation going forward.

We highlighted that the school paid $15,928 for the principal and her partner to attend the 4-day Innovative Schools Summit in Las Vegas in the United States. While the trip had a business purpose consistent with the school’s strategy, it was not clear that all the expenditure incurred as part of the trip had a business purpose, in particular the school paying for the principal’s partners' travel, a stopover in San Francisco, and tourist activities during the trip. The school’s policies require all spending to be clearly linked to the business of the school. Where private travel is combined with work travel the expectation is that there is no additional cost to the organisation and the private travel is only incidental to the business purpose of the travel.

We highlighted that the school breached the law by failing to obtain specific approval from the Secretary of Education for a payment over $25,000 made to a board member or an entity in which they have an interest in the financial year. Subsequent to the breach, the school has initiated a retrospective approval application on the advice of the Ministry of Education Finance Advisor.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school is experiencing serious financial difficulties. The school has incurred several deficits over recent years and relies on support from the Ministry of Education to meet its obligations as they fall due.
We highlighted that the school incurred costs of $9,327 for the principal’s international travel. The school's financial management policies and procedures were not followed.

We highlighted that the school paid $4,369 for flights and accommodation for the principal and a companion to Queenstown from the school's 2024 professional coaching and wellbeing support funding. However, the school was unable  to provide evidence of the business purpose for this spending, and how it related to the school's objectives. Paying for personal travel of a staff member or other travelling companion is not consistent with the school’s travel policy. In addition, the Board has not requested concurrence from the Ministry of Education for the additional benefit that the principal received.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school is experiencing serious financial difficulties. The school has incurred a deficit during the year and relies on support from the Ministry of Education to meet its obligations as they fall due.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining the uncertainty because some of the school's buildings have been identified by the Ministry of Education to have weathertightness issues. While the school continues to set funds aside in line with its previous painting plan, it is uncertain about how the Ministry of Education’s weathertightness project will affect the school and the property maintenance it is obliged to carry out.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school is experiencing serious financial difficulties. The school has incurred several deficits over recent years and relies on support from the Ministry of Education to meet its obligations as they fall due.

We highlighted that the school approved and paid $2,620 for travel and hospitality for the principal and their family from the school's 2024 professional coaching and wellbeing support funding. However, there was no clear business purpose for this spending, and paying for personal travel of a staff member and their family is not consistent with the school’s travel policy. In addition, the Board has not requested concurrence from the Ministry of Education for the additional benefit that the principal received.

We drew attention to disclosures outlining that the school spent $369,846 on a trip to the United States of America, for 44 students and adults. This cost included travel fees, accommodation, meals and other entertainment costs. The trip was funded by student contributions, fundraising and community grants. With the board's approval the principal withdrew $44,554 and spent $38,882 on the students’ meals and entertainment costs, depositing the remainder ($5,672) in the school's bank account after the trip. The school was unable to provide sufficient evidence for this expenditure or verify the completeness of the remaining funds redeposited, because there were no receipts for the spending. The school failed to comply with the law by not keeping accounting records that correctly explain the transactions of the school.

We highlighed that the school spent $12,046 for wellness-related activities and services in 2023. Eligible staff could claim up to $550. However, as the school did not have a a policy setting out what activities and services were allowable, it was not possible to assess whether the funds have been spent in accordance with the school’s policies, and the expectations for the use of public funds. Furthermore, the Board has not requested concurrence from the Ministry of Education to provide additional benefits to staff above those set out in the collective agreements.

School: Te Kura Kaupapa Maori O Mangere
Description 1: We highlighted that the kura spent $35,026 on hospitality and entertainment for the school staff, and $3,000 on sponsorship for two ex-students during 2023. While the spending on hospitality was mostly related to the business purpose of the school, this included $22,833 on a boat cruise for 45 people and other expenditure which we would not consider to be either moderate or conservative.

School: Te Kura Kaupapa Maori o Te Tonga o Hokianga
Description 1: We highlighted that the school paid $6,000 for a personal trip for the principal to Turkey from the school's 2024 professional coaching and wellbeing support funding. The Board considered that the trip contributed towards the principal's wellbeing, but there was no clear business purpose for this spending. Paying for personal travel of a staff member is not consistent with the school’s travel policy. In addition, the Board has not requested concurrence from the Ministry of Education for the additional benefit that the principal received. We also highlighted that the school decided to donate a van purchased in 10 January 2023 to a local club, incurring a loss on disposal of $29,994.

We highlighted that the school spent $11,440 ($2,875  was expensed in the previous year) for the principal and his familyto travel to Rarotonga where the principal attended the Learning Network conference. The school approved and paid for the flights of the principal's family on the understanding that the flights would be reimbursed. At the date of our audit report the amounts have not been paid back. Paying for personal travel of a staff member and their family is not consistent with the school’s travel policy or with the expectations for the use of public funds. Where personal travel is incorporated with business travel this should be at no additional cost to the organisation.

We highlighted that the Board paid $6,000 to the previous principal as reimbursement for personal travel using the professional coaching and wellbeing support funding for principals. The funds had not been spent in accordance with the school’s policies on professional development and sensitive expenditure, and the expectations for the use of public funds. Also, the Board has not requested concurrence from the Ministry of Education for the additional payment or benefit that the principal received.

The School has recognised a provision for cyclical maintenance to reflect the expected future cost of maintaining school buildings, based on the 10-Year Property Plan (10YPP) prepared in November 2023. The School subsequently obtained updated cyclical maintenance calculations  in 2024. However, there is a significant variance between these estimates, and the accuracy of the updated calculations remains uncertain at the date of approving these financial statements. As a result, we were unable to determine whether any adjustments to the cyclical maintenance provision were necessary.

Our audit was limited because we were unable to obtain enough appropriate evidence to determine whether the provision for cyclical maintenance was complete. The school did not have a current plan for cyclical maintenance. Due to the absence of evidence, it is possible that this provision could be materially misstated.

We disagreed with the Board not having a provision for cyclical maintenance. This is a departure from the relevant financial reporting standard, which requires that a provision be recorded where there is a present obligation that will be required to be settled, and the obligation can be reliably estimated. The Board has not calculated the financial effect of this departure from the reporting standard.

We disagreed with the Board for not preparing and annually review a property plan that includeds all of the maintenance requirements over a ten-year period according to the Integration Agreement between the Proprietor and the Ministry of Education. As a result, the Board did not record a provision for cyclical maintenance.
